summary: Hildegard of Bingen was a 12th-century Benedictine abbess, mystic, and polymath who viewed the earth as a living, sacred organism animated by a divine 'greening power' (viriditas). She taught that humanity is a microcosm of the universe, intimately connected to nature, and called to co-create with the natural world. Her visions, experienced in a fully conscious state, emphasized the feminine aspects of divine wisdom and the profound interconnectedness of all creation.

principles.statement: The earth is a sacred, living organism that sustains humanity and must be protected.
principles.rationale: The earth is endowed with the same vital, divine power that animates all life forms. Injuring it harms the divine creation that sustains us.
principles.source_quote: The earth sustains humanity. It must not be injured; it must not be destroyed.

principles.statement: Humanity must actively co-create with the natural world.
principles.rationale: By working cooperatively with nature and cultivating the earthly, we bring about heavenly conditions on earth.
principles.source_quote: Humankind is called to co-create, so that we might cultivate the earthly, and thereby create the heavenly.

principles.statement: Every aspect of creation reflects the divine presence.
principles.rationale: The beauty and fecundity of nature are participants in the holy chorus of creation, underpinned by a divine force.
principles.source_quote: Every creature is a glittering, glistening mirror of divinity

mental_models.name: Viriditas (Greening Power)
mental_models.description: A lens that views the divine as a vibrant, nourishing energy that enlivens both body and soul, symbolized by the color green, ripening, and the fecundity of nature.
mental_models.example: She envisioned this force as the breeze that nurtures green things and the rain that causes grass to laugh with the joy of life.

mental_models.name: The Tree of the Soul
mental_models.description: A metaphor comparing the human soul to the sap within a tree, illustrating how spiritual understanding grows and vitalizes the body just as sap greens the branches and leaves.
mental_models.example: The soul is in the body, just like the sap is in the tree. Understanding grows in the soul, just like the greening of branches and the leaves of the tree.

mental_models.name: Humanity as Microcosm
mental_models.description: The concept that a human being contains the entirety of the cosmos—heaven, earth, and all creation—within themselves.
mental_models.example: Humanity, take a good look at yourself. Inside, you’ve got heaven and earth, and all of creation. You’re a world—everything is hidden in you.

heuristics: Pay close attention to the rhythms of nature, because it holds the secret to our physical well-being and to the vitality of an inner life.

signature_quotes.text: The earth sustains humanity. It must not be injured; it must not be destroyed.
signature_quotes.context: Declaring her reverence for the earth and its vital power.

signature_quotes.text: Every creature is a glittering, glistening mirror of divinity
signature_quotes.context: Describing her inclusive vision of nature and the divine.

signature_quotes.text: Creation is the song of God
signature_quotes.context: Observing the wonder and splendor of nature's divine underpinning.

signature_quotes.text: Humankind is called to co-create, so that we might cultivate the earthly, and thereby create the heavenly.
signature_quotes.context: Urging her nuns to become partners with the natural world.

signature_quotes.text: Humanity, take a good look at yourself. Inside, you’ve got heaven and earth, and all of creation. You’re a world—everything is hidden in you.
signature_quotes.context: Expressing optimism about the possibility of men and women becoming fully realized human beings.

signature_quotes.text: The soul is in the body, just like the sap is in the tree.
signature_quotes.context: Using a tree as a metaphor for the growth of the soul.

anti_patterns.description: Tithing young children to the religious life.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Children are too young and innocent to be required to make such a lifelong commitment, a practice she herself was subjected to and later criticized.

anti_patterns.description: Suppressing one's inner truth and visions out of fear of ridicule.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Yielding to self-doubt and the fear of others' opinions cripples the individual and can lead to physical and spiritual illness, as Hildegard experienced before she finally wrote down her visions.

anti_patterns.description: Being out of sync with the beauty and fecundity of nature.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It denies the divine force (viriditas) which enlivens both the body and the soul.
